frameworks.name: continual learning
frameworks.one_line: successive multi-task learning over a lifespan without forgetting.

frameworks.name: factorized task representation
frameworks.one_line: orthogonal task-rule codes that segregate tasks to prevent interference.

frameworks.name: supervised deep neural networks
frameworks.one_line: standard backprop nets suffer catastrophic forgetting under blocked training.

frameworks.name: unsupervised generative embedding
frameworks.one_line: pre-learning a stimulus-space embedding mitigates interference in deep nets.

core_question: Why can humans learn successive tasks without forgetting, and what design lets neural networks do the same?
key_assumptions: task rules can be orthogonal over a shared stimulus space
individuals differ in prior tendency to structure the stimulus space
interference arises in deeper representational layers
manipulations: training regime: blocked vs interleaved exposure to two orthogonal rules
agent type: humans vs standard deep nets vs deep nets augmented with generative embedding
key_findings: blocked training improved later interleaved-test performance in humans relative to interleaved control
human error patterns indicated factorized task representations under blocked training
the factorization benefit was stronger in individuals with structured stimulus-space priors
standard deep nets showed catastrophic forgetting under blocked training; an unsupervised generative pre-embedding reduced it
interpretations: blocked exposure promotes orthogonal task codes that protect prior knowledge from interference
a learned stimulus-space embedding is a key ingredient for human-like continual learning in machines
